# COMP382 HW7: NP-Completeness Reductions

## Student Responses

<!-- Write your solutions below. Add sections for each problem as needed. -->
<!-- Use proper mathematical notation where applicable. -->

## Problem 1: Efficient Recruiting

We reduce **Vertex Cover** to **Efficient Recruiting**.

### Vertex Cover instance

Let the input be an undirected graph \(G=(V,E)\) and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(C \subseteq V\) with \(|C| \le k\) such that every edge in \(E\) has at least one endpoint in \(C\).

### Construction of Efficient Recruiting instance

We build an Efficient Recruiting instance as follows.

- Create one applicant for each vertex in \(V\).
- Create one sport for each edge in \(E\).
- For an edge \(e=\{u,v\}\), declare exactly the two applicants corresponding to \(u\) and \(v\) to be qualified for the sport corresponding to \(e\).
- Keep the same integer \(k\).

This construction is clearly polynomial in the size of the input graph.

### Correctness

We show that
\[
(G,k) \in \text{Vertex Cover} \iff (I,k) \in \text{Efficient Recruiting},
\]
where \(I\) is the constructed recruiting instance.

#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If \(G\) has a vertex cover of size at most \(k\), then the recruiting instance has a feasible hiring set of size at most \(k\).

Assume \(C \subseteq V\) is a vertex cover with \(|C| \le k\). Hire the applicants corresponding to the vertices in \(C\).

Consider any sport corresponding to an edge \(e=\{u,v\}\). Since \(C\) is a vertex cover, at least one of \(u\) or \(v\) lies in \(C\). Therefore at least one hired applicant is qualified for that sport. Since this holds for every edge/sport, all sports are covered by at most \(k\) hired counselors.

#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If the recruiting instance has a feasible hiring set of size at most \(k\), then \(G\) has a vertex cover of size at most \(k\).

Assume there is a set \(H\) of at most \(k\) applicants that covers every sport. Let \(C\) be the corresponding set of vertices in \(G\).

Take any edge \(e=\{u,v\}\). The corresponding sport can be covered only by the applicants for \(u\) or \(v\), because those are exactly the applicants qualified for that sport. Since \(H\) covers that sport, at least one of \(u\) or \(v\) is in \(C\). Hence every edge has an endpoint in \(C\), so \(C\) is a vertex cover with \(|C| \le k\).

### Conclusion

The reduction is polynomial, and the constructed Efficient Recruiting instance is a yes-instance exactly when the original Vertex Cover instance is a yes-instance. Therefore,
\[
\text{Vertex Cover} \le_p \text{Efficient Recruiting}.
\]

## Problem 2: Plot Fulfillment

We reduce **Hitting Set** to **Plot Fulfillment**.

### Hitting Set instance

Let the input be a universe \(U=\{u_1,\dots,u_n\}\), a family of subsets \(S_1,\dots,S_m \subseteq U\), and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(H \subseteq U\) with \(|H| \le k\) such that \(H \cap S_j \ne \emptyset\) for every \(j\).

### Construction of Plot Fulfillment instance

Create a directed graph whose pages are arranged in \(k\) layers. Each layer contains one page for every element of \(U\).

More formally:

- For each position \(r \in \{1,\dots,k\}\) and each element \(u \in U\), create a page \(p_{r,u}\).
- Add a start page \(s\) and an end page \(t\).
- Add directed edges from \(s\) to every page in layer 1.
- For each \(r=1,\dots,k-1\), add directed edges from every page in layer \(r\) to every page in layer \(r+1\).
- Add directed edges from every page in layer \(k\) to \(t\).

Thus, every trail from \(s\) to \(t\) chooses exactly one element from each of the \(k\) layers, which can be viewed as selecting up to \(k\) elements of \(U\), with repetition allowed.

Now define the thematic-element sets. For each subset \(S_j\) in the Hitting Set instance, create a thematic set
\[
T_j = \{p_{r,u} : 1 \le r \le k,\ u \in S_j\}.
\]
So \(T_j\) contains all pages, across all layers, whose label is an element of \(S_j\).

This construction has \(kn+2\) pages and polynomially many edges, so it is polynomial-time.

### Correctness

We show that the Hitting Set instance is a yes-instance iff the constructed Plot Fulfillment instance has an \(s\)-to-\(t\) trail containing at least one page from each \(T_j\).

#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If there is a hitting set of size at most \(k\), then there is a fulfilling trail.

Assume \(H=\{h_1,\dots,h_r\}\) is a hitting set with \(r \le k\). Form a trail from \(s\) to \(t\) by choosing:

- in layer 1, the page labeled \(h_1\),
- in layer 2, the page labeled \(h_2\),
- ...
- in layer \(r\), the page labeled \(h_r\),
- in the remaining layers \(r+1,\dots,k\), any arbitrary page (for example, reuse \(h_r\) if \(r \ge 1\)).

This is a valid trail because all consecutive layers are fully connected.

Now fix any subset \(S_j\). Since \(H\) is a hitting set, some \(h_i \in H \cap S_j\). The trail includes the page \(p_{i,h_i}\), and by construction \(p_{i,h_i} \in T_j\). Therefore the trail visits at least one page from every thematic set \(T_j\).

#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If there is a fulfilling trail, then there is a hitting set of size at most \(k\).

Assume there is a trail from \(s\) to \(t\) that contains at least one page from each \(T_j\). Because the graph has \(k\) layers and edges only move forward one layer at a time, the trail selects exactly one page from each layer. Let the element labels of those \(k\) chosen pages be \(u^{(1)},\dots,u^{(k)}\), and let
\[
H = \{u^{(1)},\dots,u^{(k)}\}.
\]
Then \(|H| \le k\).

Take any subset \(S_j\). Since the trail contains some page in \(T_j\), it contains some page \(p_{r,u}\) with \(u \in S_j\). But then \(u\) is one of the chosen labels, so \(u \in H \cap S_j\). Hence \(H\) intersects every \(S_j\), so \(H\) is a hitting set of size at most \(k\).

### Conclusion

The reduction is polynomial and preserves yes-instances, so
\[
\text{Hitting Set} \le_p \text{Plot Fulfillment}.
\]

## Problem 3: Path Selection

We reduce **Independent Set** to **Path Selection**.

### Independent Set instance

Let the input be an undirected graph \(G=(V,E)\) and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(I \subseteq V\) with \(|I| \ge k\) such that no two vertices in \(I\) are adjacent.

### Construction of Path Selection instance

Suppose \(E=\{e_1,\dots,e_m\}\). We build a directed graph \(G'=(V',E')\) and one requested path \(P_v\) for each vertex \(v \in V\).

For each edge \(e_j\), create a node \(x_j\) in \(G'\).

For each vertex \(v \in V\), define a path \(P_v\) that visits exactly the nodes \(x_j\) such that edge \(e_j\) is incident to \(v\), in increasing order of \(j\). To make this a genuine directed path, add directed edges between consecutive nodes on that list.

If a vertex \(v\) is isolated, create a private node \(y_v\) and let \(P_v\) be the single-node path \(y_v\).

Thus:

- the request set contains one path \(P_v\) for each vertex \(v \in V\),
- two paths share a node exactly when the corresponding vertices share an incident edge,
- the target number remains \(k\).

This construction is polynomial in \(|V|+|E|\).

### Why the construction works

For distinct vertices \(u,v \in V\):

- If \(u\) and \(v\) are adjacent in \(G\), then they share some edge \(e_j\). By construction, both \(P_u\) and \(P_v\) contain node \(x_j\). So the two requested paths are not allowed to be chosen together.
- If \(u\) and \(v\) are not adjacent in \(G\), then there is no edge incident to both. Therefore there is no node \(x_j\) appearing in both \(P_u\) and \(P_v\), so the two paths are node-disjoint.

Hence the intersection graph of the requested paths is exactly the original graph \(G\).

### Correctness

We now prove
\[
(G,k) \in \text{Independent Set} \iff (G',\{P_v\}_{v \in V},k) \in \text{Path Selection}.
\]

#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) Independent set implies selectable node-disjoint paths

Assume \(I \subseteq V\) is an independent set with \(|I| \ge k\). Consider the collection of path requests \(\{P_v : v \in I\}\).

Since no two vertices in \(I\) are adjacent, no two corresponding paths share a node. Therefore these paths are pairwise node-disjoint, and at least \(k\) requests can be selected.

#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) Selectable node-disjoint paths imply independent set

Assume at least \(k\) requested paths can be selected so that no two share a node. Let \(S\) be the corresponding set of vertices of \(G\).

If two vertices \(u,v \in S\) were adjacent in \(G\), then the paths \(P_u\) and \(P_v\) would share the node corresponding to edge \(\{u,v\}\), contradicting node-disjointness. Therefore no two vertices in \(S\) are adjacent, so \(S\) is an independent set with \(|S| \ge k\).

### Conclusion

The reduction is polynomial and correct, therefore
\[
\text{Independent Set} \le_p \text{Path Selection}.
\]

## Problem 4: Half 3-SAT Satisfiability

We reduce **3-SAT** to **Half 3-SAT Satisfiability**.

### 3-SAT instance

Let
\[
\varphi = C_1 \land C_2 \land \cdots \land C_m
\]
be a 3-CNF formula. We may assume \(m \ge 1\).

### Construction

Introduce one fresh variable \(y\), and construct
\[
\varphi' \;=\; \varphi \;\land\; (y \lor \neg y \lor y) \;\land\; \bigwedge_{j=1}^{m+1} (y \lor y \lor y).
\]

So \(\varphi'\) contains:

- the original \(m\) clauses of \(\varphi\),
- one tautological 3-clause \((y \lor \neg y \lor y)\), which is always true,
- \(m+1\) copies of the 3-clause \((y \lor y \lor y)\), which is true exactly when \(y=\text{true}\).

Hence the total number of clauses in \(\varphi'\) is
\[
m + 1 + (m+1) = 2m+2,
\]
which is even, as required.

The construction is clearly polynomial-time.

### Correctness

We show
\[
\varphi \in \text{3-SAT} \iff \varphi' \in \text{Half 3-SAT}.
\]

#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If \(\varphi\) is satisfiable, then \(\varphi'\) has an assignment with exactly half the clauses true.

Assume \(\varphi\) is satisfiable. Take an assignment that satisfies all \(m\) original clauses, and extend it by setting
\[
y = \text{false}.
\]

Under this assignment:

- all \(m\) original clauses are true,
- the tautology \((y \lor \neg y \lor y)\) is true,
- each of the \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) is false.

So the number of true clauses is
\[
m + 1.
\]
Since the total number of clauses is \(2m+2\), exactly half are true. Therefore \(\varphi'\) is a yes-instance of Half 3-SAT.

#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If \(\varphi'\) has an assignment with exactly half the clauses true, then \(\varphi\) is satisfiable.

Assume there is an assignment that makes exactly half of the \(2m+2\) clauses of \(\varphi'\) true. Then exactly
\[
\frac{2m+2}{2} = m+1
\]
clauses are true.

We claim that this assignment must set \(y=\text{false}\). If \(y=\text{true}\), then:

- the tautology is true,
- all \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) are true.

That already gives
\[
1 + (m+1) = m+2
\]
true clauses, which is more than \(m+1\). This is impossible. Therefore \(y=\text{false}\).

If \(y=\text{false}\), then:

- all \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) are false,
- the tautology is still true.

So among the \(m\) original clauses plus the tautology, exactly \(m+1\) clauses must be true. Since the tautology contributes 1 of those, all \(m\) original clauses must be true. Hence the restriction of this assignment to the original variables satisfies \(\varphi\).

### Conclusion

The mapping is polynomial and preserves yes-instances in both directions, so
\[
\text{3-SAT} \le_p \text{Half 3-SAT Satisfiability}.
\]

# COMP382 HW7: NP-Completeness Reductions

## Student Responses

<!-- Write your solutions below. Add sections for each problem as needed. -->
<!-- Use proper mathematical notation where applicable. -->

## Problem 1: Efficient Recruiting

We reduce **Vertex Cover** to **Efficient Recruiting**.

### Vertex Cover instance

Let the input be an undirected graph \(G=(V,E)\) and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(C \subseteq V\) with \(|C| \le k\) such that every edge in \(E\) has at least one endpoint in \(C\).

### Construction of Efficient Recruiting instance

We build an Efficient Recruiting instance as follows.

- Create one applicant for each vertex in \(V\).
- Create one sport for each edge in \(E\).
- For an edge \(e=\{u,v\}\), declare exactly the two applicants corresponding to \(u\) and \(v\) to be qualified for the sport corresponding to \(e\).
- Keep the same integer \(k\).

This construction is clearly polynomial in the size of the input graph.

### Correctness

We show that
\[
(G,k) \in \text{Vertex Cover} \iff (I,k) \in \text{Efficient Recruiting},
\]
where \(I\) is the constructed recruiting instance.

#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If \(G\) has a vertex cover of size at most \(k\), then the recruiting instance has a feasible hiring set of size at most \(k\).

Assume \(C \subseteq V\) is a vertex cover with \(|C| \le k\). Hire the applicants corresponding to the vertices in \(C\).

Consider any sport corresponding to an edge \(e=\{u,v\}\). Since \(C\) is a vertex cover, at least one of \(u\) or \(v\) lies in \(C\). Therefore at least one hired applicant is qualified for that sport. Since this holds for every edge/sport, all sports are covered by at most \(k\) hired counselors.

#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If the recruiting instance has a feasible hiring set of size at most \(k\), then \(G\) has a vertex cover of size at most \(k\).

Assume there is a set \(H\) of at most \(k\) applicants that covers every sport. Let \(C\) be the corresponding set of vertices in \(G\).

Take any edge \(e=\{u,v\}\). The corresponding sport can be covered only by the applicants for \(u\) or \(v\), because those are exactly the applicants qualified for that sport. Since \(H\) covers that sport, at least one of \(u\) or \(v\) is in \(C\). Hence every edge has an endpoint in \(C\), so \(C\) is a vertex cover with \(|C| \le k\).

### Conclusion

The reduction is polynomial, and the constructed Efficient Recruiting instance is a yes-instance exactly when the original Vertex Cover instance is a yes-instance. Therefore,
\[
\text{Vertex Cover} \le_p \text{Efficient Recruiting}.
\]

## Problem 2: Plot Fulfillment

We reduce **Hitting Set** to **Plot Fulfillment**.

### Hitting Set instance

Let the input be a universe \(U=\{u_1,\dots,u_n\}\), a family of subsets \(S_1,\dots,S_m \subseteq U\), and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(H \subseteq U\) with \(|H| \le k\) such that \(H \cap S_j \ne \emptyset\) for every \(j\).

### Construction of Plot Fulfillment instance

Create a directed graph whose pages are arranged in \(k\) layers. Each layer contains one page for every element of \(U\).

More formally:

- For each position \(r \in \{1,\dots,k\}\) and each element \(u \in U\), create a page \(p_{r,u}\).
- Add a start page \(s\) and an end page \(t\).
- Add directed edges from \(s\) to every page in layer 1.
- For each \(r=1,\dots,k-1\), add directed edges from every page in layer \(r\) to every page in layer \(r+1\).
- Add directed edges from every page in layer \(k\) to \(t\).

Thus, every trail from \(s\) to \(t\) chooses exactly one element from each of the \(k\) layers, which can be viewed as selecting up to \(k\) elements of \(U\), with repetition allowed.

Now define the thematic-element sets. For each subset \(S_j\) in the Hitting Set instance, create a thematic set
\[
T_j = \{p_{r,u} : 1 \le r \le k,\ u \in S_j\}.
\]
So \(T_j\) contains all pages, across all layers, whose label is an element of \(S_j\).

This construction has \(kn+2\) pages and polynomially many edges, so it is polynomial-time.

### Correctness

We show that the Hitting Set instance is a yes-instance iff the constructed Plot Fulfillment instance has an \(s\)-to-\(t\) trail containing at least one page from each \(T_j\).

#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If there is a hitting set of size at most \(k\), then there is a fulfilling trail.

Assume \(H=\{h_1,\dots,h_r\}\) is a hitting set with \(r \le k\). Form a trail from \(s\) to \(t\) by choosing:

- in layer 1, the page labeled \(h_1\),
- in layer 2, the page labeled \(h_2\),
- ...
- in layer \(r\), the page labeled \(h_r\),
- in the remaining layers \(r+1,\dots,k\), any arbitrary page (for example, reuse \(h_r\) if \(r \ge 1\)).

This is a valid trail because all consecutive layers are fully connected.

Now fix any subset \(S_j\). Since \(H\) is a hitting set, some \(h_i \in H \cap S_j\). The trail includes the page \(p_{i,h_i}\), and by construction \(p_{i,h_i} \in T_j\). Therefore the trail visits at least one page from every thematic set \(T_j\).

#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If there is a fulfilling trail, then there is a hitting set of size at most \(k\).

Assume there is a trail from \(s\) to \(t\) that contains at least one page from each \(T_j\). Because the graph has \(k\) layers and edges only move forward one layer at a time, the trail selects exactly one page from each layer. Let the element labels of those \(k\) chosen pages be \(u^{(1)},\dots,u^{(k)}\), and let
\[
H = \{u^{(1)},\dots,u^{(k)}\}.
\]
Then \(|H| \le k\).

Take any subset \(S_j\). Since the trail contains some page in \(T_j\), it contains some page \(p_{r,u}\) with \(u \in S_j\). But then \(u\) is one of the chosen labels, so \(u \in H \cap S_j\). Hence \(H\) intersects every \(S_j\), so \(H\) is a hitting set of size at most \(k\).

### Conclusion

The reduction is polynomial and preserves yes-instances, so
\[
\text{Hitting Set} \le_p \text{Plot Fulfillment}.
\]

## Problem 3: Path Selection

We reduce **Independent Set** to **Path Selection**.

### Independent Set instance

Let the input be an undirected graph \(G=(V,E)\) and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(I \subseteq V\) with \(|I| \ge k\) such that no two vertices in \(I\) are adjacent.

### Construction of Path Selection instance

Suppose \(E=\{e_1,\dots,e_m\}\). We build a directed graph \(G'=(V',E')\) and one requested path \(P_v\) for each vertex \(v \in V\).

For each edge \(e_j\), create a node \(x_j\) in \(G'\).

For each vertex \(v \in V\), define a path \(P_v\) that visits exactly the nodes \(x_j\) such that edge \(e_j\) is incident to \(v\), in increasing order of \(j\). To make this a genuine directed path, add directed edges between consecutive nodes on that list.

If a vertex \(v\) is isolated, create a private node \(y_v\) and let \(P_v\) be the single-node path \(y_v\).

Thus:

- the request set contains one path \(P_v\) for each vertex \(v \in V\),
- two paths share a node exactly when the corresponding vertices share an incident edge,
- the target number remains \(k\).

This construction is polynomial in \(|V|+|E|\).

### Why the construction works

For distinct vertices \(u,v \in V\):

- If \(u\) and \(v\) are adjacent in \(G\), then they share some edge \(e_j\). By construction, both \(P_u\) and \(P_v\) contain node \(x_j\). So the two requested paths are not allowed to be chosen together.
- If \(u\) and \(v\) are not adjacent in \(G\), then there is no edge incident to both. Therefore there is no node \(x_j\) appearing in both \(P_u\) and \(P_v\), so the two paths are node-disjoint.

Hence the intersection graph of the requested paths is exactly the original graph \(G\).

### Correctness

We now prove
\[
(G,k) \in \text{Independent Set} \iff (G',\{P_v\}_{v \in V},k) \in \text{Path Selection}.
\]

#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) Independent set implies selectable node-disjoint paths

Assume \(I \subseteq V\) is an independent set with \(|I| \ge k\). Consider the collection of path requests \(\{P_v : v \in I\}\).

Since no two vertices in \(I\) are adjacent, no two corresponding paths share a node. Therefore these paths are pairwise node-disjoint, and at least \(k\) requests can be selected.

#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) Selectable node-disjoint paths imply independent set

Assume at least \(k\) requested paths can be selected so that no two share a node. Let \(S\) be the corresponding set of vertices of \(G\).

If two vertices \(u,v \in S\) were adjacent in \(G\), then the paths \(P_u\) and \(P_v\) would share the node corresponding to edge \(\{u,v\}\), contradicting node-disjointness. Therefore no two vertices in \(S\) are adjacent, so \(S\) is an independent set with \(|S| \ge k\).

### Conclusion

The reduction is polynomial and correct, therefore
\[
\text{Independent Set} \le_p \text{Path Selection}.
\]

## Problem 4: Half 3-SAT Satisfiability

We reduce **3-SAT** to **Half 3-SAT Satisfiability**.

### 3-SAT instance

Let
\[
\varphi = C_1 \land C_2 \land \cdots \land C_m
\]
be a 3-CNF formula. We may assume \(m \ge 1\).

### Construction

Introduce one fresh variable \(y\), and construct
\[
\varphi' \;=\; \varphi \;\land\; (y \lor \neg y \lor y) \;\land\; \bigwedge_{j=1}^{m+1} (y \lor y \lor y).
\]

So \(\varphi'\) contains:

- the original \(m\) clauses of \(\varphi\),
- one tautological 3-clause \((y \lor \neg y \lor y)\), which is always true,
- \(m+1\) copies of the 3-clause \((y \lor y \lor y)\), which is true exactly when \(y=\text{true}\).

Hence the total number of clauses in \(\varphi'\) is
\[
m + 1 + (m+1) = 2m+2,
\]
which is even, as required.

The construction is clearly polynomial-time.

### Correctness

We show
\[
\varphi \in \text{3-SAT} \iff \varphi' \in \text{Half 3-SAT}.
\]

#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If \(\varphi\) is satisfiable, then \(\varphi'\) has an assignment with exactly half the clauses true.

Assume \(\varphi\) is satisfiable. Take an assignment that satisfies all \(m\) original clauses, and extend it by setting
\[
y = \text{false}.
\]

Under this assignment:

- all \(m\) original clauses are true,
- the tautology \((y \lor \neg y \lor y)\) is true,
- each of the \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) is false.

So the number of true clauses is
\[
m + 1.
\]
Since the total number of clauses is \(2m+2\), exactly half are true. Therefore \(\varphi'\) is a yes-instance of Half 3-SAT.

#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If \(\varphi'\) has an assignment with exactly half the clauses true, then \(\varphi\) is satisfiable.

Assume there is an assignment that makes exactly half of the \(2m+2\) clauses of \(\varphi'\) true. Then exactly
\[
\frac{2m+2}{2} = m+1
\]
clauses are true.

We claim that this assignment must set \(y=\text{false}\). If \(y=\text{true}\), then:

- the tautology is true,
- all \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) are true.

That already gives
\[
1 + (m+1) = m+2
\]
true clauses, which is more than \(m+1\). This is impossible. Therefore \(y=\text{false}\).

If \(y=\text{false}\), then:

- all \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) are false,
- the tautology is still true.

So among the \(m\) original clauses plus the tautology, exactly \(m+1\) clauses must be true. Since the tautology contributes 1 of those, all \(m\) original clauses must be true. Hence the restriction of this assignment to the original variables satisfies \(\varphi\).

### Conclusion

The mapping is polynomial and preserves yes-instances in both directions, so
\[
\text{3-SAT} \le_p \text{Half 3-SAT Satisfiability}.
\]

graderReview.content:
# COMP382 HW7: NP-Completeness Reductions — LLM Grading Results

## Results Summary

**Passed:** 4/4 problems (100%)  
**Points:** 100/100

## Detailed Results

### ✅ Problem 1: Efficient Recruiting (25/25 pts)

This is a **complete and correct reduction**. Your construction exactly captures the vertex-cover condition: covering every sport corresponds to choosing vertices that touch every edge. Both directions are proven cleanly, and your backward direction correctly uses the fact that only the two endpoint-applicants can cover the sport for a given edge. This matches the reference solution in substance and rigor. There are no meaningful gaps here.

### ✅ Problem 2: Plot Fulfillment (25/25 pts)

This is a **correct and well-explained reduction**. The graph construction and thematic sets align with the intended encoding of choosing up to `k` elements of the universe. Your forward direction correctly turns a hitting set into a valid trail, including the important detail of padding when the hitting set has size less than `k`. Your backward direction also correctly extracts a hitting set from the labels of the visited pages. This is fully consistent with the reference solution, just written with slightly different notation.

### ✅ Problem 3: Path Selection (25/25 pts)

This solution is **correct** and captures the same idea as the reference solution, though with a slightly different graph construction. Instead of making the new graph complete, you add only the edges needed so that each requested sequence is an actual directed path; that is perfectly valid. The central property is preserved: adjacent vertices in the original graph correspond to paths that share the node for their common edge, while nonadjacent vertices correspond to node-disjoint paths. The isolated-vertex case is also handled cleanly. This deserves full credit.

### ✅ Problem 4: Half 3-SAT Satisfiability (25/25 pts)

This is a **fully correct alternative reduction** to the one in the reference solution. Your gadget is simpler in one sense: the tautological clause contributes a guaranteed `1`, and the `m+1` copies of `(y \lor y \lor y)` force the half-satisfied threshold to encode full satisfiability of the original formula. The converse argument is rigorous: if `y=true`, too many clauses become true; if `y=false`, then hitting exactly half requires all original clauses to be true. This is an excellent reduction and merits full credit.

## Overall Comments

The submission is **consistently strong across all four problems**. You chose the correct reduction direction every time, which is the most important structural requirement in NP-completeness proofs. In each problem, you gave a valid construction, proved both directions of the `iff`, and noted polynomial-time computability.

A notable strength is that your proofs are not just intuitive; they explicitly track why a yes-instance in the source problem corresponds to a yes-instance in the target problem. Problem 4 is especially good because you used a correct reduction different from the reference solution while maintaining full rigor. Overall, this is a mathematically correct and well-presented homework.

---

# COMP382 HW7: NP-Completeness Reductions — LLM Grading Results

## Results Summary

**Passed:** 4/4 problems (100%)  
**Points:** 100/100

## Detailed Results

### ✅ Problem 1: Efficient Recruiting (25/25 pts)

This is a **complete and correct reduction**. Your construction exactly captures the vertex-cover condition: covering every sport corresponds to choosing vertices that touch every edge. Both directions are proven cleanly, and your backward direction correctly uses the fact that only the two endpoint-applicants can cover the sport for a given edge. This matches the reference solution in substance and rigor. There are no meaningful gaps here.

### ✅ Problem 2: Plot Fulfillment (25/25 pts)

This is a **correct and well-explained reduction**. The graph construction and thematic sets align with the intended encoding of choosing up to `k` elements of the universe. Your forward direction correctly turns a hitting set into a valid trail, including the important detail of padding when the hitting set has size less than `k`. Your backward direction also correctly extracts a hitting set from the labels of the visited pages. This is fully consistent with the reference solution, just written with slightly different notation.

### ✅ Problem 3: Path Selection (25/25 pts)

This solution is **correct** and captures the same idea as the reference solution, though with a slightly different graph construction. Instead of making the new graph complete, you add only the edges needed so that each requested sequence is an actual directed path; that is perfectly valid. The central property is preserved: adjacent vertices in the original graph correspond to paths that share the node for their common edge, while nonadjacent vertices correspond to node-disjoint paths. The isolated-vertex case is also handled cleanly. This deserves full credit.

### ✅ Problem 4: Half 3-SAT Satisfiability (25/25 pts)

This is a **fully correct alternative reduction** to the one in the reference solution. Your gadget is simpler in one sense: the tautological clause contributes a guaranteed `1`, and the `m+1` copies of `(y \lor y \lor y)` force the half-satisfied threshold to encode full satisfiability of the original formula. The converse argument is rigorous: if `y=true`, too many clauses become true; if `y=false`, then hitting exactly half requires all original clauses to be true. This is an excellent reduction and merits full credit.

## Overall Comments

The submission is **consistently strong across all four problems**. You chose the correct reduction direction every time, which is the most important structural requirement in NP-completeness proofs. In each problem, you gave a valid construction, proved both directions of the `iff`, and noted polynomial-time computability.

A notable strength is that your proofs are not just intuitive; they explicitly track why a yes-instance in the source problem corresponds to a yes-instance in the target problem. Problem 4 is especially good because you used a correct reduction different from the reference solution while maintaining full rigor. Overall, this is a mathematically correct and well-presented homework.

# COMP382 HW7: NP-Completeness Reductions — LLM Grading Results

## Results Summary

**Passed:** 4/4 problems (100%)  
**Points:** 100/100

## Detailed Results

### ✅ Problem 1: Efficient Recruiting (25/25 pts)

This is a **flawless solution**. The reduction is the natural and correct one: vertices become counselors, edges become sports, and the qualification relation mirrors edge-endpoint incidence. Both directions of the correctness proof are rigorous and leave no gaps. The reverse direction is especially well-handled by explicitly noting that the only applicants qualified for a sport corresponding to edge \{u,v\} are the applicants for u and v, which is the key step that makes the argument airtight.

### ✅ Problem 2: Plot Fulfillment (25/25 pts)

This is an **excellent and complete solution**. The layered graph construction is the standard and correct approach. The student's presentation is arguably more detailed than the reference solution in several places:

- The handling of |H| < k in the forward direction is made explicit (fill remaining layers with an arbitrary repeated element), which the reference somewhat glosses over.
- The backward direction carefully argues that the trail structure forces exactly one page per layer, correctly bounding |H| ≤ k.
- The thematic set definition correctly spreads each subset S_j across all layers, ensuring that any element of S_j chosen at any layer position satisfies the thematic requirement.

No errors or gaps detected.

### ✅ Problem 3: Path Selection (25/25 pts)

This is a **correct and well-structured solution**. The student's construction differs slightly from the reference (which uses a complete directed graph) but is equally valid and in some ways cleaner:

- The reference builds G' as the complete directed graph on edge-nodes and defines paths through it. The student instead adds only the directed edges needed to make each P_v a valid path. Both approaches work.
- The student's choice to order edge-nodes by index ensures no directional conflicts when multiple paths share edge-nodes, since all paths traverse shared nodes in the same direction.
- The isolated vertex handling with private nodes is a nice detail that the reference omits.
- The core equivalence — **node-sharing ⟺ adjacency** — is proved clearly and correctly in both directions.

This is a fully correct alternative to the reference construction.

### ✅ Problem 4: Half 3-SAT Satisfiability (25/25 pts)

This is a **correct and elegant solution** that uses a different (and arguably more efficient) construction than the reference. The reference pads with 4m total clauses using m always-true clauses and 2m y-controlled clauses, while the student uses only 2m+2 clauses with a single tautology and m+1 y-controlled clauses. Both are valid.

**Key verification of the student's construction:**

- **Forward:** φ satisfiable → set y = false → m original true + 1 tautology true + 0 y-clauses true = m+1 = (2m+2)/2 ✓
- **Backward, y = true:** 1 tautology + (m+1) y-clauses = m+2 > m+1 already, contradiction ✓
- **Backward, y = false:** 0 y-clauses true, 1 tautology true, so need all m originals true → φ satisfiable ✓

The logic is impeccable throughout. This is a fully correct alternative reduction.

## Overall Comments

This is an **outstanding submission** — all four reductions are correct, complete, and rigorously proved.

**Patterns of strength:**
- Every problem includes a clear construction, explicit bidirectional correctness proof, and polynomial-time argument
- The mathematical writing is precise and well-organized throughout
- Several solutions show independent thinking beyond the reference: Problem 3 uses a minimal directed graph rather than a complete graph, Problem 4 uses a more efficient construction (2m+2 vs. 4m clauses), and Problem 3 handles the isolated-vertex edge case
- The backward directions are argued especially carefully, with key constraints (e.g., which applicants can cover a sport, why y must be false) made explicit

**No significant errors detected in any problem.** The student demonstrates strong mastery of polynomial-time reductions and NP-completeness proof techniques.

 <!-- Use proper mathematical notation where applicable. -->
 
-[Your solutions here]
+## Problem 1: Efficient Recruiting
 
+We reduce **Vertex Cover** to **Efficient Recruiting**.
+
+### Vertex Cover instance
+
+Let the input be an undirected graph \(G=(V,E)\) and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(C \subseteq V\) with \(|C| \le k\) such that every edge in \(E\) has at least one endpoint in \(C\).
+
+### Construction of Efficient Recruiting instance
+
+We build an Efficient Recruiting instance as follows.
+
+- Create one applicant for each vertex in \(V\).
+- Create one sport for each edge in \(E\).
+- For an edge \(e=\{u,v\}\), declare exactly the two applicants corresponding to \(u\) and \(v\) to be qualified for the sport corresponding to \(e\).
+- Keep the same integer \(k\).
+
+This construction is clearly polynomial in the size of the input graph.
+
+### Correctness
+
+We show that
+\[
+(G,k) \in \text{Vertex Cover} \iff (I,k) \in \text{Efficient Recruiting},
+\]
+where \(I\) is the constructed recruiting instance.
+
+#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If \(G\) has a vertex cover of size at most \(k\), then the recruiting instance has a feasible hiring set of size at most \(k\).
+
+Assume \(C \subseteq V\) is a vertex cover with \(|C| \le k\). Hire the applicants corresponding to the vertices in \(C\).
+
+Consider any sport corresponding to an edge \(e=\{u,v\}\). Since \(C\) is a vertex cover, at least one of \(u\) or \(v\) lies in \(C\). Therefore at least one hired applicant is qualified for that sport. Since this holds for every edge/sport, all sports are covered by at most \(k\) hired counselors.
+
+#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If the recruiting instance has a feasible hiring set of size at most \(k\), then \(G\) has a vertex cover of size at most \(k\).
+
+Assume there is a set \(H\) of at most \(k\) applicants that covers every sport. Let \(C\) be the corresponding set of vertices in \(G\).
+
+Take any edge \(e=\{u,v\}\). The corresponding sport can be covered only by the applicants for \(u\) or \(v\), because those are exactly the applicants qualified for that sport. Since \(H\) covers that sport, at least one of \(u\) or \(v\) is in \(C\). Hence every edge has an endpoint in \(C\), so \(C\) is a vertex cover with \(|C| \le k\).
+
+### Conclusion
+
+The reduction is polynomial, and the constructed Efficient Recruiting instance is a yes-instance exactly when the original Vertex Cover instance is a yes-instance. Therefore,
+\[
+\text{Vertex Cover} \le_p \text{Efficient Recruiting}.
+\]
+
+## Problem 2: Plot Fulfillment
+
+We reduce **Hitting Set** to **Plot Fulfillment**.
+
+### Hitting Set instance
+
+Let the input be a universe \(U=\{u_1,\dots,u_n\}\), a family of subsets \(S_1,\dots,S_m \subseteq U\), and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(H \subseteq U\) with \(|H| \le k\) such that \(H \cap S_j \ne \emptyset\) for every \(j\).
+
+### Construction of Plot Fulfillment instance
+
+Create a directed graph whose pages are arranged in \(k\) layers. Each layer contains one page for every element of \(U\).
+
+More formally:
+
+- For each position \(r \in \{1,\dots,k\}\) and each element \(u \in U\), create a page \(p_{r,u}\).
+- Add a start page \(s\) and an end page \(t\).
+- Add directed edges from \(s\) to every page in layer 1.
+- For each \(r=1,\dots,k-1\), add directed edges from every page in layer \(r\) to every page in layer \(r+1\).
+- Add directed edges from every page in layer \(k\) to \(t\).
+
+Thus, every trail from \(s\) to \(t\) chooses exactly one element from each of the \(k\) layers, which can be viewed as selecting up to \(k\) elements of \(U\), with repetition allowed.
+
+Now define the thematic-element sets. For each subset \(S_j\) in the Hitting Set instance, create a thematic set
+\[
+T_j = \{p_{r,u} : 1 \le r \le k,\ u \in S_j\}.
+\]
+So \(T_j\) contains all pages, across all layers, whose label is an element of \(S_j\).
+
+This construction has \(kn+2\) pages and polynomially many edges, so it is polynomial-time.
+
+### Correctness
+
+We show that the Hitting Set instance is a yes-instance iff the constructed Plot Fulfillment instance has an \(s\)-to-\(t\) trail containing at least one page from each \(T_j\).
+
+#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If there is a hitting set of size at most \(k\), then there is a fulfilling trail.
+
+Assume \(H=\{h_1,\dots,h_r\}\) is a hitting set with \(r \le k\). Form a trail from \(s\) to \(t\) by choosing:
+
+- in layer 1, the page labeled \(h_1\),
+- in layer 2, the page labeled \(h_2\),
+- ...
+- in layer \(r\), the page labeled \(h_r\),
+- in the remaining layers \(r+1,\dots,k\), any arbitrary page (for example, reuse \(h_r\) if \(r \ge 1\)).
+
+This is a valid trail because all consecutive layers are fully connected.
+
+Now fix any subset \(S_j\). Since \(H\) is a hitting set, some \(h_i \in H \cap S_j\). The trail includes the page \(p_{i,h_i}\), and by construction \(p_{i,h_i} \in T_j\). Therefore the trail visits at least one page from every thematic set \(T_j\).
+
+#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If there is a fulfilling trail, then there is a hitting set of size at most \(k\).
+
+Assume there is a trail from \(s\) to \(t\) that contains at least one page from each \(T_j\). Because the graph has \(k\) layers and edges only move forward one layer at a time, the trail selects exactly one page from each layer. Let the element labels of those \(k\) chosen pages be \(u^{(1)},\dots,u^{(k)}\), and let
+\[
+H = \{u^{(1)},\dots,u^{(k)}\}.
+\]
+Then \(|H| \le k\).
+
+Take any subset \(S_j\). Since the trail contains some page in \(T_j\), it contains some page \(p_{r,u}\) with \(u \in S_j\). But then \(u\) is one of the chosen labels, so \(u \in H \cap S_j\). Hence \(H\) intersects every \(S_j\), so \(H\) is a hitting set of size at most \(k\).
+
+### Conclusion
+
+The reduction is polynomial and preserves yes-instances, so
+\[
+\text{Hitting Set} \le_p \text{Plot Fulfillment}.
+\]
+
+## Problem 3: Path Selection
+
+We reduce **Independent Set** to **Path Selection**.
+
+### Independent Set instance
+
+Let the input be an undirected graph \(G=(V,E)\) and an integer \(k\). The question is whether there exists a set \(I \subseteq V\) with \(|I| \ge k\) such that no two vertices in \(I\) are adjacent.
+
+### Construction of Path Selection instance
+
+Suppose \(E=\{e_1,\dots,e_m\}\). We build a directed graph \(G'=(V',E')\) and one requested path \(P_v\) for each vertex \(v \in V\).
+
+For each edge \(e_j\), create a node \(x_j\) in \(G'\).
+
+For each vertex \(v \in V\), define a path \(P_v\) that visits exactly the nodes \(x_j\) such that edge \(e_j\) is incident to \(v\), in increasing order of \(j\). To make this a genuine directed path, add directed edges between consecutive nodes on that list.
+
+If a vertex \(v\) is isolated, create a private node \(y_v\) and let \(P_v\) be the single-node path \(y_v\).
+
+Thus:
+
+- the request set contains one path \(P_v\) for each vertex \(v \in V\),
+- two paths share a node exactly when the corresponding vertices share an incident edge,
+- the target number remains \(k\).
+
+This construction is polynomial in \(|V|+|E|\).
+
+### Why the construction works
+
+For distinct vertices \(u,v \in V\):
+
+- If \(u\) and \(v\) are adjacent in \(G\), then they share some edge \(e_j\). By construction, both \(P_u\) and \(P_v\) contain node \(x_j\). So the two requested paths are not allowed to be chosen together.
+- If \(u\) and \(v\) are not adjacent in \(G\), then there is no edge incident to both. Therefore there is no node \(x_j\) appearing in both \(P_u\) and \(P_v\), so the two paths are node-disjoint.
+
+Hence the intersection graph of the requested paths is exactly the original graph \(G\).
+
+### Correctness
+
+We now prove
+\[
+(G,k) \in \text{Independent Set} \iff (G',\{P_v\}_{v \in V},k) \in \text{Path Selection}.
+\]
+
+#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) Independent set implies selectable node-disjoint paths
+
+Assume \(I \subseteq V\) is an independent set with \(|I| \ge k\). Consider the collection of path requests \(\{P_v : v \in I\}\).
+
+Since no two vertices in \(I\) are adjacent, no two corresponding paths share a node. Therefore these paths are pairwise node-disjoint, and at least \(k\) requests can be selected.
+
+#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) Selectable node-disjoint paths imply independent set
+
+Assume at least \(k\) requested paths can be selected so that no two share a node. Let \(S\) be the corresponding set of vertices of \(G\).
+
+If two vertices \(u,v \in S\) were adjacent in \(G\), then the paths \(P_u\) and \(P_v\) would share the node corresponding to edge \(\{u,v\}\), contradicting node-disjointness. Therefore no two vertices in \(S\) are adjacent, so \(S\) is an independent set with \(|S| \ge k\).
+
+### Conclusion
+
+The reduction is polynomial and correct, therefore
+\[
+\text{Independent Set} \le_p \text{Path Selection}.
+\]
+
+## Problem 4: Half 3-SAT Satisfiability
+
+We reduce **3-SAT** to **Half 3-SAT Satisfiability**.
+
+### 3-SAT instance
+
+Let
+\[
+\varphi = C_1 \land C_2 \land \cdots \land C_m
+\]
+be a 3-CNF formula. We may assume \(m \ge 1\).
+
+### Construction
+
+Introduce one fresh variable \(y\), and construct
+\[
+\varphi' \;=\; \varphi \;\land\; (y \lor \neg y \lor y) \;\land\; \bigwedge_{j=1}^{m+1} (y \lor y \lor y).
+\]
+
+So \(\varphi'\) contains:
+
+- the original \(m\) clauses of \(\varphi\),
+- one tautological 3-clause \((y \lor \neg y \lor y)\), which is always true,
+- \(m+1\) copies of the 3-clause \((y \lor y \lor y)\), which is true exactly when \(y=\text{true}\).
+
+Hence the total number of clauses in \(\varphi'\) is
+\[
+m + 1 + (m+1) = 2m+2,
+\]
+which is even, as required.
+
+The construction is clearly polynomial-time.
+
+### Correctness
+
+We show
+\[
+\varphi \in \text{3-SAT} \iff \varphi' \in \text{Half 3-SAT}.
+\]
+
+#### (\(\Rightarrow\)) If \(\varphi\) is satisfiable, then \(\varphi'\) has an assignment with exactly half the clauses true.
+
+Assume \(\varphi\) is satisfiable. Take an assignment that satisfies all \(m\) original clauses, and extend it by setting
+\[
+y = \text{false}.
+\]
+
+Under this assignment:
+
+- all \(m\) original clauses are true,
+- the tautology \((y \lor \neg y \lor y)\) is true,
+- each of the \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) is false.
+
+So the number of true clauses is
+\[
+m + 1.
+\]
+Since the total number of clauses is \(2m+2\), exactly half are true. Therefore \(\varphi'\) is a yes-instance of Half 3-SAT.
+
+#### (\(\Leftarrow\)) If \(\varphi'\) has an assignment with exactly half the clauses true, then \(\varphi\) is satisfiable.
+
+Assume there is an assignment that makes exactly half of the \(2m+2\) clauses of \(\varphi'\) true. Then exactly
+\[
+\frac{2m+2}{2} = m+1
+\]
+clauses are true.
+
+We claim that this assignment must set \(y=\text{false}\). If \(y=\text{true}\), then:
+
+- the tautology is true,
+- all \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) are true.
+
+That already gives
+\[
+1 + (m+1) = m+2
+\]
+true clauses, which is more than \(m+1\). This is impossible. Therefore \(y=\text{false}\).
+
+If \(y=\text{false}\), then:
+
+- all \(m+1\) copies of \((y \lor y \lor y)\) are false,
+- the tautology is still true.
+
+So among the \(m\) original clauses plus the tautology, exactly \(m+1\) clauses must be true. Since the tautology contributes 1 of those, all \(m\) original clauses must be true. Hence the restriction of this assignment to the original variables satisfies \(\varphi\).
+
+### Conclusion
+
+The mapping is polynomial and preserves yes-instances in both directions, so
+\[
+\text{3-SAT} \le_p \text{Half 3-SAT Satisfiability}.
+\]
